turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson has the opinion of the court in Case 23-583, Bouarfa v. Mayorkas.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Our nation's complex system of lawful immigration commonly features mandatory statutory rules paired with discretionary exceptions. Executive branch agencies implement both. Whether any given agency decision is mandatory or discretionary matters because Congress has limited judicial review of many discretionary determinations. The agency decision at issue in this case occurs along the pathway to permanent legal status for a non-citizen spouse of the U.S. citizen. A U.S. citizen starts the process by filing a visa petition on behalf of her spouse with the U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services. If the agency approves the petition, the non-citizen may then proceed to apply for an immigrant visa to enter the country as a lawful permanent resident, or if he is already in the country, as he can seek adjustment of status. But Congress has given the Secretary of Homeland Security the power to revoke the agency's initial petition approval. Specifically in 8 U.S.C. 1155, Congress has provided that the Secretary "may at any time for what he deems to be good and sufficient cause revoke the approval of any petition."" The petitioner in this case, Amina Bouarfa, is a US citizen who filed a visa petition on behalf of her non-citizen husband, a petition that the agency initially approved. But two years later, the Secretary of Homeland Security told Bouarfa that the agency was revoking that approval because it had uncovered evidence suggesting that Bouarfa's husband had previously entered into a sham marriage. The Secretary told Bouarfa that had the agency been aware of the sham marriage when it was reviewing the Application in the first instance, it would have been required by statute to deny the petition. Bouarfa contested the secretary's conclusion that her husband had previously entered into a sham marriage, but the secretary stuck to the agency's determination and deemed the sham marriage good cause to revoke the agency's approval of her petition. The issue in this case is whether an 1155 revocation of approval of a visa petition based on a sham marriage determination qualifies as a decision in the discretion of the secretary, such that it falls within the purview of a separate statute that strips federal courts of jurisdiction to review certain discretionary agency actions. We hold that it does. It is clear on the face of the revocation statute that 1155 is a quintessential grant of discretion to the secretary. The statute uses language of a type that we have previously held clearly connotes discretion insofar as it provides that the secretary may revoke the approval of a visa petition at any time for reasons he deems to be good and sufficient and Congress in no way prescribed how that discretion must be exercised. There are no conditions that the secretary must satisfy to access his revocation power. Bouarfa responds that although many revocations are discretionary, the revocation here was mandatory because the sham marriage finding is a statutory basis for mandatory denial of a petition, and the secretary thus determined that the agency's prior approval had been a mistake. But nothing about the text or context of the statutory scheme requires the agency to correct its mistakes in this way. Instead, Congress gave the secretary a choice. If he determines at any point that the agency's prior approval of a visa petition was erroneous, he is free to revoke it, or he can let the error stand. As a general matter then, this discretion can work to the benefit of some visa petition beneficiaries. In any event, when the secretary opts to revoke a petition that he determines should not have been approved in the first place, a petitioner has not run out of options. A citizen is not prohibited from filing another petition on behalf of the same relative and indeed, Bouarfa has already taken advantage of that alternative here. She has filed a second petition that is presently pending. If the agency denies that petition, Bouarfa will likely be able to receive judicial review of the sham marriage determination at that time. For these reasons and others, we lay out in the opinion we filed today, the decision of the Eleventh Circuit is affirmed. The decision is unanimous.
